version_history.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: btrfs_log_dev_io_error() on all bio errors

patches_submitted.patch_summary: The patch aims to log device I/O errors for all bio errors, not just BLK_STS_IOERR and BLK_STS_TARGET. This is done by removing the conditional checks in btrfs_log_dev_io_error() and instead asserting that the bio status is valid.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Reviewed the patch and found it to be a good change. The current implementation of error logging is indeed limited and this patch fixes that.
review_comments.raw_body:
\u5728 2026/3/14 05:56, Boris Burkov \u5199\u9053:
> As far as I can tell, we never intentionally constrained ourselves to
> these status codes, and it is misleading and surprising to lack the
> bdev error logging when we get a different error code from the block
> layer. This can lead to jumping to a wrong conclusion like "this
> system didn't see any bio failures but aborted with EIO".
> 
> For example on nvme devices, I observe many failures coming back as
> BLK_STS_MEDIUM. It is apparent that the nvme driver returns a variety of
> BLK_STS_* status values in nvme_error_status().
> 
> Signed-off-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>

Reviewed-by: Qu Wenruo <wqu@suse.com>

Thanks,
Qu


> ---
>   fs/btrfs/bio.c | 3 +--
>   1 file changed, 1 insertion(+), 2 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/fs/btrfs/bio.c b/fs/btrfs/bio.c
> index 2a2a21aec817..08b1d62603d0 100644
> --- a/fs/btrfs/bio.c
> +++ b/fs/btrfs/bio.c
> @@ -352,8 +352,7 @@ static void btrfs_log_dev_io_error(const struct bio *bio, struct btrfs_device *d
>   {
>   	if (!dev || !dev->bdev)
>   		return;
> -	if (bio->bi_status != BLK_STS_IOERR && bio->bi_status != BLK_STS_TARGET)
> -		return;
> +	ASSERT(bio->bi_status);
>   
>   	if (btrfs_op(bio) == BTRFS_MAP_WRITE)
>   		btrfs_dev_stat_inc_and_print(dev, BTRFS_DEV_STAT_WRITE_ERRS);

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Suggested leaving the current implementation as-is for now, but promised to discuss and address the block status vs file system issues soon.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, Mar 13, 2026 at 12:26:05PM -0700, Boris Burkov wrote:
> As far as I can tell, we never intentionally constrained ourselves to
> these status codes, and it is misleading and surprising to lack the
> bdev error logging when we get a different error code from the block
> layer. This can lead to jumping to a wrong conclusion like "this
> system didn't see any bio failures but aborted with EIO".
> 
> For example on nvme devices, I observe many failures coming back as
> BLK_STS_MEDIUM. It is apparent that the nvme driver returns a variety of
> BLK_STS_* status values in nvme_error_status().

This goes all the way back to adding the error counts in commit
442a4f6308e69.  The block error values are a bit of a mess and it's
on my todo list to sort them out - only a small number of them should
even be seen here.  I'd suggest to leave things as-is for now, and
I'll make sure you're included in the discussion when I get around
to attack the block status vs file system issues, which will hopefully
be very soon.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov
review_comments.summary: Responded to Christoph's suggestion by pointing out that logging the errors they are already seeing would be beneficial. Offered to help ensure btrfs does what Christoph wants when he addresses the block status issues.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, Mar 16, 2026 at 05:20:20PM +0100, Christoph Hellwig wrote:
> On Fri, Mar 13, 2026 at 12:26:05PM -0700, Boris Burkov wrote:
> > As far as I can tell, we never intentionally constrained ourselves to
> > these status codes, and it is misleading and surprising to lack the
> > bdev error logging when we get a different error code from the block
> > layer. This can lead to jumping to a wrong conclusion like "this
> > system didn't see any bio failures but aborted with EIO".
> > 
> > For example on nvme devices, I observe many failures coming back as
> > BLK_STS_MEDIUM. It is apparent that the nvme driver returns a variety of
> > BLK_STS_* status values in nvme_error_status().
> 
> This goes all the way back to adding the error counts in commit
> 442a4f6308e69.  The block error values are a bit of a mess and it's
> on my todo list to sort them out - only a small number of them should
> even be seen here.  I'd suggest to leave things as-is for now, and
> I'll make sure you're included in the discussion when I get around
> to attack the block status vs file system issues, which will hopefully
> be very soon.
> 

Is there a reason not to properly log the errors we are definitely
already seeing? I have thousands of instances with swallowed
BLK_STS_MEDIUM errors that really confused me and messed up my data
while investigating a possible regression.

If you think this will confuse/upset users who aren't watching a giant
fleet with distributed logging and make them throw out good drives
prematurely or something, then I can just carry this patch internally.

If you are just worried about having to aim at a moving target, I am
happy to offer to be available to make sure btrfs does what you want
when you do find time to work on this. It's hard for me to judge since I
don't really know what is wrong with the error signalling as-is.

Put another way, if we are in a situation with technical debt, which is
what I think you are saying, then I would rather have more visibility
rather than less.

Thanks,
Boris

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/2] locking: contended_release tracepoint instrumentation
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new tracepoint called contended_release that fires on the lock holder's side when a lock is released while there are waiters. This provides additional information such as hold time estimation and the holder's stack at release time, which can be useful for debugging and performance analysis. The new tracepoint has minimal impact on performance and binary size. It was previously discussed in an RFC (Request For Comments) and this patch addresses some of the feedback received.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Usama Arif pointed out that the patch does not consistently check for blocked waiters, specifically suggesting to use waitqueue_active(&sem->waiters) in percpu_up_write() to match other calls.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hello!

I saw that you mentioned in the commmit message that you do this for only
blocked waiters except for percpu_up_write(). We can use
waitqueue_active(&sem->waiters) to check for this over here so that
its consistent with every other call?

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Gave Acked-by

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author acknowledged a potential issue with using waitqueue_active() in the tracepoint instrumentation, agreeing that wq_has_sleeper() is more suitable since it doesn't require holding the lock and adding a barrier.

Author acknowledged a concern about the timing of the contended_release tracepoint and moved it to fire after the lock is released but before rcuwait_wake_up(), agreeing to restructure in v3
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for the feedback, Usama.

I thought about it and even mentioned in the comment, but I forgot what
was the reason. Now, I think you are correct. I added wq_has_sleeper()
locally instead of waitqueue_active() locally, since we are not holding
the lock here and waitqueue_active() requires a barrier based on the
comment. It might be not very important here, but I'd rather make it
correct even for tracepoint.

Note that __percpu_up_read() doesn't need this guard. Maybe I was
thinking at __percpu_up_read() part before and just made it symmetric.

Anyway, thanks for suggestion.

---

Good point. I moved it after this_cpu_dec() so the tracepoint fires
after the lock is released but before rcuwait_wake_up(). I also went
through all other call sites and made the placement consistent where
possible: after release, before wake. It should be fixed in v3.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 2/2] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new tracepoint called contended_release, which fires when a lock is released under contention. The patch complements existing contention_begin and contention_end tracepoints by providing information from the holder's perspective. It enables correlating lock hold time under contention with waiter events by lock address. The patch introduces calls to trace_contended_release() in slowpath unlock paths of various sleepable locks, including mutex, rtmutex, semaphore, rwsem, percpu-rwsem, and RT-specific rwbase locks.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 1/2] EVM: add comment describing why ino field is still unsigned long
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a comment to the EVM code explaining why the inode number field is still an unsigned long, despite the inode->i_ino being widened to u64. series_items.patch_summary: The patch removes unnecessary static variables in the nfsd_fh expkey lookup function, converting them to local variables scoped to the outer for loop.

series_items.patch_summary: This patch series removes outdated documentation from the exportfs manpage, specifically legacy mode information for 2.4 and earlier kernels that used the nfsservctl() syscall.

series_items.patch_summary: This patch series updates the netlink headers for all cache upcalls in the nfs-utils package, syncing them with the current kernel UAPI header and adding a new bundled sunrpc_netlink.h header for the sunrpc genl family.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils 05/17] build: add libnl3 and netlink header support for exportd and mountd
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for libnl3 and netlink headers to the exportd and mountd builds in nfs-utils. It updates the configure.ac file to include checks for libnl3, sunrpc_netlink.h, and linux/nfsd_netlink.h. The changes ensure that the system header includes expkey support by checking for NFSD_A_EXPKEY_SEQNO.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils 06/17] xlog: claim D_FAC3 as D_NETLINK

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils 07/17] exportd/mountd: add netlink support for svc_export cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds netlink-based support for the svc_export cache, allowing userspace to receive notifications of pending export requests from the kernel and respond accordingly. The existing procfs channel mechanism is retained as a fallback in case the kernel does not support the nfsd genetlink family or the 'exportd' multicast group.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the potential for duplicate processing of export requests and suggested adding a check to prevent it. Also asked for more information on how the netlink setup silently fails in older kernels.

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils 08/17] exportd/mountd: add netlink support for the nfsd.fh cache

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils 09/17] exportd/mountd: add netlink support for the auth.unix.ip cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series refactors the netlink support for the auth.unix.ip cache in the NFS-utils codebase, introducing a generic helper function and converting existing code to use it. The sunrpc genl family socket setup is also added for handling upcalls from the kernel.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Identified potential issues with the patch series, including missing error handling and unclear code paths. Requested clarification on the purpose of the generic helper function and suggested improvements to the existing code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils 10/17] exportd/mountd: add netlink support for the auth.unix.gid cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds netlink support for the auth.unix.gid cache in the NFS-utils exportd and mountd components. The goal is to improve performance by reducing the number of getpwuid() and getgrouplist() calls. The patch introduces a new handler that resolves UIDs to group lists using netlink messages.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils 11/17] mountd/exportd: only use /proc interfaces if netlink setup fails
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series modifies the mountd and exportd components of nfs-utils to use netlink interfaces instead of /proc channel files, except in cases where netlink setup fails.

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils 12/17] support/export: check for pending requests after opening netlink sockets
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series introduces support for checking pending requests after opening netlink sockets in the NFS-utils code. The goal is to ensure that any queued requests are processed before mountd comes online.

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils 13/17] exportd/mountd: use cache type from notifications to target scanning
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the exportd/mountd components of the nfs-utils package to use cache type information from netlink multicast notifications to target scanning. It parses NFSD_A_CACHE_NOTIFY_CACHE_TYPE and SUNRPC_A_CACHE_NOTIFY_CACHE_TYPE attributes, determines which caches have pending requests, and only issues GET_REQS for those caches, reducing unnecessary netlink round-trips.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils 14/17] nfsd/sunrpc: add cache flush command and attribute enums
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for flushing nfsd and sunrpc caches via generic netlink commands, allowing userspace to selectively flush specific caches instead of writing to /proc/net/rpc/* files.

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils 15/17] exportfs: add netlink support for cache flush with /proc fallback
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds netlink support for cache flush operations in the exportfs module, providing a fallback to /proc/net/rpc/*/flush if the sunrpc genl family cannot be resolved on older kernels.

series_items.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils 16/17] exportfs: use netlink to probe kernel support, skip export_test
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the exportfs module to use netlink to probe kernel support for new cache interfaces, allowing it to skip an advisory export test.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 14/14] nfsd: add NFSD_CMD_CACHE_FLUSH netlink command

series_items.subject: [PATCH 00/14] nfsd/sunrpc: add support for netlink upcalls for mountd/exportd
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for netlink upcalls to the NFS server and client, replacing antiquated interfaces in /proc. The new mechanism uses a generic netlink family and allows for more flexible and efficient handling of cache updates.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: There is no review comment from Jeff Layton in the provided thread.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 01/14] nfsd: move struct nfsd_genl_rqstp to nfsctl.c
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the definition of struct nfsd_genl_rqstp from fs/nfsd/nfsd.h to fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c, where it is used. This change aims to keep related definitions together in a single file.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 02/14] sunrpc: rename sunrpc_cache_pipe_upcall() to sunrpc_cache_upcall()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the function sunrpc_cache_pipe_upcall() to sunrpc_cache_upcall() in preparation for its future use of netlink for upcalls, allowing it to be more flexible and adaptable.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential issue with the rename, suggesting that it might be better to introduce a new function for netlink upcalls instead of modifying an existing one.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 03/14] sunrpc: rename sunrpc_cache_pipe_upcall_timeout()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the function sunrpc_cache_pipe_upcall_timeout() to sunrpc_cache_upcall_warn(), as it no longer represents a timeout but instead warns if there are no listeners.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: The function name was misleading and did not accurately reflect its purpose. The rename to sunrpc_cache_upcall_warn() is a good correction.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 04/14] sunrpc: rename cache_pipe_upcall() to cache_do_upcall()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the cache_pipe_upcall() function to cache_do_upcall() in preparation for handling netlink upcalls, which will also be handled by this function.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: The patch is a simple rename of the function, which will be used for both pipe and netlink upcalls.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 05/14] sunrpc: add a cache_notify callback
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new cache_notify operation to struct cache_detail, which will be used by a later patch to send netlink notifications when there is a pending cache_request.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 06/14] sunrpc: add helpers to count and snapshot pending cache requests
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds helpers to count and snapshot pending cache requests in the sunrpc subsystem, allowing callers to safely access the request list without exposing internal struct cache_request details.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the use of cache_get() reference in sunrpc_cache_requests_snapshot(), suggesting an alternative approach to ensure safe access to the request list.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 07/14] sunrpc: add a generic netlink family for cache upcalls
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series introduces a generic netlink family for cache upcalls in the sunrpc module, addressing the limitation of existing caches living within the module. It defines a YAML spec and implements the necessary code to support cache notifications.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the implementation of sunrpc_cache_notify() and suggested using a more generic approach to handle cache notifications.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 08/14] sunrpc: add netlink upcall for the auth.unix.ip cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds netlink-based cache upcall support for the ip_map (auth.unix.ip) cache, including new attributes and operations in the sunrpc_cache YAML spec, and updates to the net/sunrpc/netlink.c and net/sunrpc/svcauth_unix.c files.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: No comments from David Hildenbrand in the thread so far.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 09/14] sunrpc: add netlink upcall for the auth.unix.gid cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds netlink-based cache upcall support for the unix_gid (auth.unix.gid) cache in the sunrpc subsystem. It introduces a new attribute-set and operations to manage pending requests and responses, and wires up a callback to trigger multicast events on cache misses.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the implementation of sunrpc_nl_unix_gid_get_reqs_dumpit() and suggested a different approach to handling pending requests.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 10/14] nfsd: add new netlink spec for svc_export upcall
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new netlink specification for NFSd to handle svc_export upcalls, including definitions and attribute sets for cache notify, RPC status, file system location, authentication flavor, and service export requests.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the patch's complexity and potential impact on performance, suggesting a more incremental approach to implementing netlink support for NFSd.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 11/14] nfsd: add netlink upcall for the svc_export cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds netlink-based cache upcall support for the svc_export (nfsd.export) cache, enabling userspace to receive notifications about pending requests and updates.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the implementation of nfsd_cache_notify() and suggested alternative approaches to handle cache notifications.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 12/14] nfsd: add netlink upcall for the nfsd.fh cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds netlink-based cache upcall support for the nfsd.fh cache, including a new expkey attribute-set and operations to handle client requests and updates.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: The patch series implements netlink-based cache upcall support for the nfsd.fh cache, including a new expkey attribute-set and operations to handle client requests and updates. The code adds a new enum value for expkey, a new attribute-set with fields for client, fsidtype, fsid, negative flag, expiry, and path, and two new operations to dump pending expkey requests and parse responses from userspace.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 13/14] sunrpc: add SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_FLUSH netlink command
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_FLUSH generic netlink command to flush sunrpc auth caches without writing to /proc/net/rpc/*/flush. The command allows userspace to select which caches to flush using an optional attribute.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new NFSD_CMD_CACHE_FLUSH generic netlink command to allow userspace to flush nfsd export caches without writing to /proc/net/rpc/*/flush. The command takes an optional u32 attribute to select which caches to flush, and falls back to the /proc-based cache_flush() for older kernels.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: No substantive comments from David Hildenbrand in the thread.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v5 1/4] openat2: new OPENAT2_REGULAR flag support
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch adds a new flag called OPENAT2_REGULAR to the openat2() function, which restricts file opens to regular files only and returns an error for non-regular files. The patch also includes filesystem-specific checks in ceph, gfs2, nfs, and cifs to ensure that resources are properly cleaned up when returning errors. However, two issues were found: the flag leaks into f_flags and a BUILD_BUG_ON check was not updated. These issues need to be fixed before the patch can be applied.

review_comments.author: Dorjoy Chowdhury
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Dorjoy Chowdhury agreed that the issues found by Claude were valid and suggested specific modifications to fix them: adding OPENAT2_REGULAR to the flags cleared in do_dentry_open, and updating VALID_OPEN_FLAGS to VALID_OPENAT2_FLAGS in fs/open.c and fs/fcntl.c
review_comments.raw_body:
Good catches! I guess for issue 1 I need to modify the line in
do_dentry_open implementation to
f->f_flags &= ~(O_CREAT | O_EXCL | O_NOCTTY | O_TRUNC | OPENAT2_REGULAR);
right?
And for issue 2, I should change the VALID_OPEN_FLAGS to
VALID_OPENAT2_FLAGS in both build_open_flags in fs/open.c and in
fcntl_init in fs/fcntl.c, correct?

Regards,
Dorjoy

patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for netlink-based upcalls and downcalls in mountd, exportd, and exportfs. It refactors existing code to use the new netlink API and adds several bugfixes and cleanups.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: The patch series is a significant refactoring of existing code to use the new netlink API. The changes are extensive and cover multiple components, including mountd, exportd, and exportfs.
review_comments.raw_body:
Move lockd_netlink.h and nfsd_netlink.h from utils/nfsdctl/ to
support/include/ so they are available to other components.

The support/include/ directory is already in the default include path
(via AC_CONFIG_HEADERS), so no source-level #include changes are
needed.
---
 support/include/Makefile.am                        | 6 ++++--
 {utils/nfsdctl => support/include}/lockd_netlink.h | 0
 {utils/nfsdctl => support/include}/nfsd_netlink.h  | 0
 3 files changed, 4 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)

diff --git a/support/include/Makefile.am b/support/include/Makefile.am
index 631a84f808ae9f2a13d9539c70c09670c5ef0ff2..2b45e56b044c35c0e7a11aec5f583d600fcae6a0 100644
--- a/support/include/Makefile.am
+++ b/support/include/Makefile.am
@@ -4,13 +4,16 @@ SUBDIRS = nfs rpcsvc sys
 
 noinst_HEADERS = \
 	cld.h \
+	conffile.h \
 	exportfs.h \
 	ha-callout.h \
 	junction.h \
+	lockd_netlink.h \
 	misc.h \
 	nfs_mntent.h \
 	nfs_paths.h \
 	nfs_ucred.h \
+	nfsd_netlink.h \
 	nfsd_path.h \
 	nfslib.h \
 	nfsrpc.h \
@@ -26,7 +29,6 @@ noinst_HEADERS = \
 	xlog.h \
 	xmalloc.h \
 	xcommon.h \
-	xstat.h \
-	conffile.h
+	xstat.h
 
 MAINTAINERCLEANFILES = Makefile.in
diff --git a/utils/nfsdctl/lockd_netlink.h b/support/include/lockd_netlink.h
similarity index 100%
rename from utils/nfsdctl/lockd_netlink.h
rename to support/include/lockd_netlink.h
diff --git a/utils/nfsdctl/nfsd_netlink.h b/support/include/nfsd_netlink.h
similarity index 100%
rename from utils/nfsdctl/nfsd_netlink.h
rename to support/include/nfsd_netlink.h

-- 
2.53.0

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: The patch author has addressed minor issues in the follow-up messages, but no substantive replies have been made yet.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCHBLIZZARD v7] fuse/libfuse/e2fsprogs: containerize ext4 for safer operation
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch proposes containerizing ext4 for safer operation, allowing for isolation and security benefits without requiring near-native performance. The approach involves using lklfuse, which is a user-space filesystem that uses the Linux kernel's virtual machine (VM) to run a kernel instance in user space. This method has pros such as easier setup and maintenance compared to implementing a fuse-iomap server for every filesystem, but may have worse performance. If near-native performance is not a requirement, using lklfuse seems like a less painful option.

review_comments.author: Darrick Wong
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Darrick Wong noted that lklfuse uses user-mode-linux (UML) based on arch/lkl/Kconfig, and suggested an alternative approach by modifying libguestfs to run the fuse server inside a systemd container instead of directly from the CLI.

reviewer clarified that near-native performance is not a strict requirement for the patch's use cases, and suggested that lklfuse might be a more maintainable option if performance is not critical

reviewer noted that a good non-iomap fuse server for every filesystem would be beneficial, and expressed concern about the robustness of libext2fs compared to the kernel

Reviewer noted that using lklfuse would require running a full IO stack, potentially increasing complexity and overhead.

Reviewer noted that btrfs copy-on-write can be handled with fuse-iomap, but other features such as fsverity, fscrypt, and data checksumming are not currently supported by iomap.

Reviewer Darrick Wong expressed skepticism about the potential benefits of using lklfuse, suggesting that it may be easier to maintain than fuse-iomap but also implying that its performance might not be significantly worse.
review_comments.raw_body:
I was under the impression that lklfuse uses UML.  Given the weird
things in arch/lkl/Kconfig:

config 64BIT
	bool "64bit kernel"
	default y if OUTPUT_FORMAT = "pe-x86-64"
	default $(success,$(srctree)/arch/lkl/scripts/cc-objdump-file-format.sh|grep -q '^elf64-') if OUTPUT_FORMAT != "pe-x86-64"

I was kinda guessing x86_64 was the primary target of the developers?

/me notes that he's now looked into libguestfs per Demi Marie's comments
and some curiosity on the part of ngompa and i>

Whatever it is that libguestfs does to stand up unprivileged fs mounts
also could fit this bill.  It's *really* slow to start because it takes
the booted kernel, creates a largeish initramfs, boots that combo via
libvirt, and then fires up a fuse server to talk to the vm kernel.

I think all you'd have to do is change libguestfs to start the VM and
run the fuse server inside a systemd container instead of directly from
the CLI.

---

Not a hard requirement, just a means to convince people that they can
choose containment without completely collapsing performance.

---

Or even a good non-iomap fuse server for every filesystem.  Admittedly
the weak part of fuse4fs is that libext2fs is not as robust as the
kernel is.

---

Probably a lot, because now you have to run a full IO stack all the way
through lklfuse.

---

btrfs cow can be done with iomap, at least on the directio end.  It's
the other features like fsverity/fscrypt/data checksumming that aren't
currently supported by iomap.

---

"Gee, what if I could simplify most of my own work out of existence?"

--D

review_comments.author: Demi Obenour
review_comments.summary:
reviewer emphasized the importance of near-native performance, citing a loop where kernel devs advise against mounting untrusted filesystems but distros continue to do so due to user needs

Reviewer noted that strong verified boot requirements, such as those used by Android and Chromium OS, necessitate mounting the writable partition in userspace to prevent compromised system state on reboot.
sentiment_signals: considered being able to write corrupted metadata to disk a vulnerability
review_comments.raw_body:
It should be good enough that distros are willing to use it by default.
Otherwise, there is no way to break out of the loop where kernel devs
say to not mount untrusted filesystems, yet the distros keep doing
that because people have stuff they need to get done.

I know from talking to Neal Gompa that libguestfs is too slow for this.

---

Anyone with strong verified boot requirements is going to want this.
The threat model used by Android and Chromium OS is that all on-disk
state is *never* trusted.  The root filesystem is on a dm-verity
protected partition, so mounting it in the kernel is fine.  However,
the writable partition should be mounted in userspace.

I checked with the ChromeOS security team, and they do consider being
able to write corrupted metadata to disk that compromises the system
on the next reboot to be a vulnerability.

This is also a case where performance actually matters, including
for F2FS on zoned UFS which is always copy-on-write for user data.
-- 
Sincerely,
Demi Marie Obenour (she/her/hers)

review_comments.author: Demi Obenour
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that libguestfs is too slow for distros, making it unsuitable for mounting untrusted disk images and CI/filesystem testing

reviewer noted that on ChromeOS and Android, only read-only disk images are trusted, making a full fsck at each boot unacceptable due to slow boot times

Reviewer Demi Obenour questioned the performance impact of using lklfuse, asking for a clearer assessment of its performance implications and whether it would be noticeable in normal use.
review_comments.raw_body:
The feedback I have gotten from ngompa is that libguestfs is just
too slow for distros to use it to mount stuff.

---

It's worth noting that on ChromeOS and Android, the only trusted
disk images are those that are read-only and protected by dm-verity.
*Every* writable image is considered untrusted.

I don't know if doing a full fsck at each boot is considered
acceptable, but I suspect it would slow boot far too much.

Yes, Google ought to be paying for the kernel changes to fix this mess.

---

How much is "a lot"?  Is it "this is only useful for non-interactive
overnight backups", "you will notice this in benchmarks but it's okay
for normal use", or somewhere in between?

Could lklfuse and iomap be combined?
